original_abstract: Within the theological landscape of Afrikaans-speaking South Africa, Rudolf Bultmann’s work—particularly his program of demythologization—was historically met with significant skepticism and little positive regard. More than seventy years have passed since his seminal 1941 essay ignited a global debate, and for many contemporary scholars, Bultmann’s legacy has largely faded into the periphery of academic discourse. However, the 130th anniversary of his birth provides a timely opportunity to revisit this influential text. This paper seeks to engage directly with Bultmann’s original voice, setting aside decades of secondary interpretations to offer a fresh perspective. Now that the dust has settled on the controversies of the mid-twentieth century, a re-examination may reveal that Bultmann’s insights remain surprisingly relevant for our current theological context.
humanized_abstract: Rudolf Bultmann’s demythologization program had never been favourably received within the Evangelical world of Afrikaans South Africa, and by extension had not been taken seriously. Almost 70 years have now passed since his 1941 treatise on demythologization, sparked a tumultuous debate world-wide, and Bultmann’s name is barely mentioned in contemporary scholarship. This article now offers a timely appraisal in light of Bultmann’s 130th birthday. By engaging afresh with the words of Bultmann, irrespective of the scholarship of the intervening decades, it hopes to make a contribution to a re-exploration of his demythologization programme in a world far removed from the theological debate of mid-century. The intervening years have given sufficient time for the issues of that debate to settle, and one of the purposes of this article is to enquire into whether Bultmann’s contributions to the discussion are in any way enduring, and therefore relevant for contemporary reflection.
